items.title: AC verify report uses literal \n instead of newlines
items.intent: AC verify report must render with real newlines so validator receives properly formatted output
items.description:
run_ac_verification() builds the report string using '
' in bash double-quoted strings (e.g. report="${report}  PASS: $ac_text
"). In bash, '
' inside double quotes is a literal backslash-n, not a newline. The garbled report is injected into validator prompts and retry context, degrading AI quality.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find run_ac_verification() (around line 1218)
Locate all lines building the report variable with string concatenation using \\n
Replace \\"\\n\\" inside double-quoted strings with $\x27\\n\x27 (ANSI-C quoting) or use printf to append newlines
Verify the fix does not affect other uses of the report variable
Run a sprint with AC verify commands and confirm report output contains real newlines
acceptanceCriteria: AC verify report passed to validator contains actual newlines (not literal \\n)
Retry prompt with AC failure context displays properly formatted multi-line output
items.groomingNotes: Confirmed: bash '\n' in double-quoted strings does not produce newlines. Fix is straightforward — change to $'\n' or printf. Low risk, small scope.

items.title: Merge conflict breaks multi-sprint loop without logging skipped items
items.intent: When a merge conflict aborts a multi-sprint run, the output must clearly list which items were not attempted
items.description: In cmd_run(), when _handle_sprint_success() returns non-zero (merge conflict), the loop breaks immediately. Items queued but not yet attempted are silently abandoned. The batch summary only covers items that were started. This makes it hard to know what needs to be re-run after resolving the conflict.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find the main sprint loop in cmd_run() that calls _handle_sprint_success()
Before the loop, build an ordered list of all items that will be attempted in this batch
On break (merge conflict), compute the set difference: all queued items minus started items
Log each not-attempted item ID with a clear message before breaking
Ensure print_batch_summary or a post-loop block surfaces the skipped items count and IDs
Test with two queued items where the first causes a merge conflict
acceptanceCriteria: After a merge conflict aborts a multi-sprint run, output lists item IDs that were queued but not started
Batch summary accurately reflects items not attempted due to early abort
items.groomingNotes: Real UX gap: users lose track of which items were skipped after a merge conflict abort. Needs pre-loop item list and post-abort diff reporting.

items.title: Dead if/else branch in AC JSON construction is misleading
items.intent: AC JSON construction code must not have dead branches that confuse maintainers
items.description: In cmd_backlog_edit(), the AC entry builder has an if/else that checks whether an entry starts with '{' (JSON object) vs plain string, but both branches execute the identical statement: ac_json="$ac_json$entry". The distinction is never acted upon. This is dead/misleading code that could hide future bugs if someone adds logic to only one branch.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find the AC JSON construction block in cmd_backlog_edit() (around line 3688)
Identify the if/else that checks whether an entry starts with "{" — both branches do the same thing
Remove the if/else and keep just the single statement: ac_json="$ac_json$entry"
Verify that backlog edit --ac and --ac-verify still produce valid JSON items
Run .aishore/aishore backlog check on an item with AC entries to confirm no regression
acceptanceCriteria: The if/else branch is removed, only the single common statement remains
backlog edit --ac and --ac-verify still produce valid JSON items
backlog check on an item with AC entries shows no regression
items.groomingNotes: Tech debt: dead if/else branches mislead future developers. Trivial fix — remove the conditional.

items.title: AC verify command output truncated at 1000 bytes, may cut mid-character
items.intent: AC verification failure output must be complete enough to diagnose the problem
items.description: run_ac_verification() uses 'head -c 1000' to cap output from AC verify commands. This can cut multi-byte UTF-8 characters in half, producing garbled text. Even for ASCII, 1000 bytes is often too small for test output. The truncated output is injected into prompts, where incomplete stack traces or error messages reduce the AI's ability to fix issues.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find run_ac_verification() where it uses head -c 1000 to cap output
Replace byte-based truncation (head -c 1000) with line-based truncation (e.g. tail -n 50 or head -n 50)
Or use a higher byte limit (e.g. 5000) that is unlikely to split useful error output
Consider adding a truncation indicator (e.g. "[... output truncated]") so the AI knows output was cut
Verify AC verify failure output is readable in retry prompts after the change
acceptanceCriteria: AC verify output is not split mid-character (use line-based truncation instead of byte truncation)
Truncation limit is configurable or set to a value adequate for typical test output (e.g., 3000 chars)
items.groomingNotes: PO review: bumped to should. Truncated error output directly hurts sprint recovery — the AI has less signal to fix failures. More failures = less value delivered. 1000 byte limit is too small for real test output and the byte-split-char bug is a correctness issue.

items.title: config.yaml silently ignored when yq unavailable and no warning shown
items.intent: Users must be clearly warned when their config.yaml changes are being silently ignored due to missing yq
items.description: load_config() falls back to grep-based parsing when yq is not installed. This fallback is much less reliable and can silently ignore valid configuration. The warning is only triggered if uncommented settings are detected by grep. A config file that uses valid YAML but is structured in a way grep misses will be silently ignored, leaving the user thinking their overrides are active when they are not.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find load_config() where yq availability is checked (around line 267)
Add an early check: if config.yaml exists and has non-empty content, and yq is not available, always emit a warning
Warning should name the config file, explain what is being ignored, and give install instructions for yq
Ensure the warning is shown regardless of whether grep finds any settings
Test by creating a config.yaml with a setting and running aishore without yq installed
acceptanceCriteria: When yq is absent and config.yaml exists with content, a clear warning is always shown regardless of grep detection
Warning text explains what yq is and how to install it
items.groomingNotes: PO re-review (failCount: 2): Two sprint attempts rejected. The validator has confirmed: the warning must fire unconditionally when config.yaml exists with non-empty content and yq is absent. The grep-detection fallback MUST NOT gate the warning — this was the failure mode in both attempts. Implementation checklist: (1) check if yq is absent, (2) check if config.yaml exists and is non-empty, (3) if both true, print warning immediately before any grep fallback logic runs. Do not mix the warning into the grep-detection branch. The warning is unconditional; grep-based fallback is optional and may still run after warning. Must fix before new-user onboarding can be considered reliable.

items.title: backlog add and edit have no --step flag for setting implementation steps
items.intent: Users must be able to add and replace implementation steps via CLI without hand-editing JSON — steps are required for sprint readiness and a missing flag forces a manual workaround.
items.description: backlog add creates items with steps: [] and backlog edit has no flag to append or replace steps. The only way to populate the steps array is to edit the backlog JSON directly, which violates the CLI-first design. Both commands already follow a consistent flag pattern (--ac for AC entries, --scope for scope patterns). Steps should follow the same pattern: --step adds one step (repeatable), --clear-steps resets the array. This unblocks grooming items without requiring JSON editing.
acceptanceCriteria: backlog add --step 'Do X' creates an item with steps: ["Do X"]
backlog add --step 'Do X' --step 'Do Y' creates steps in order
backlog edit <ID> --step 'Do X' appends X to the existing steps array
backlog edit <ID> --clear-steps resets steps to empty array
items.groomingNotes: New item added during groom session. Implementation pattern mirrors --ac/--scope: local steps array, --step appends one entry per flag call, --clear-steps resets. In cmd_backlog_add(): add local steps_entries=() array, --step flag appends to it, build steps_json from the array, pass --argjson steps_arr to add_item, set steps: $steps_arr (replacing the hardcoded steps: []). In cmd_backlog_edit(): add --step flag that accumulates entries same as --ac, build steps JSON, use jq_updates += '| .steps = ((.steps // []) + $newsteps)'. Add --clear-steps: jq_updates += '| .steps = []'. --ready flag warning about no steps is expected (bootstrapping issue).

items.title: backlog show renders AC {text, verify} objects as raw JSON instead of plain text
items.intent: backlog show must display acceptance criteria as readable text — when entries are {text, verify} objects, only the text should be shown, not the JSON structure.
items.description: When backlog edit --ac 'Some check' --ac-verify 'make test' is used, the AC entry is stored as a JSON object {"text": "Some check", "verify": "make test"}. In cmd_backlog_show(), the jq display expression at line ~3720 uses (.acceptanceCriteria // [] | .[] | "  - \(.)"), which calls tojson on the entire object, producing output like: '  - {"text":"Some check","verify":"make test"}'. The fix is to check entry type: if it's a string, show it as-is; if it's an object, extract .text (and optionally show the verify command in a shorter form).
acceptanceCriteria: backlog show <ID> displays plain-string AC entries unchanged
backlog show <ID> displays {text, verify} AC entries as the .text value only (not raw JSON)
backlog show <ID> optionally shows verify command on same or next line in a readable format
items.groomingNotes: Fix location: cmd_backlog_show() jq expression around line 3720. Current: (.acceptanceCriteria // [] | .[] | "  - \(.)"). Fix: (.acceptanceCriteria // [] | .[] | "  - \(if type == "object" then .text + (if .verify then " [verify: \(.verify)]" else "" end) else . end)"). Run: backlog add with --ac 'plain' --ac 'text' --ac-verify 'cmd' then backlog show to confirm. This is a display-only fix — no data changes.

items.title: VALIDATE_CMD output injected untruncated into retry prompts
items.intent: Retry prompts must not contain unbounded validation output — token bloat degrades AI fix quality and can push critical context out of attention.
items.description: In _run_retry_loop(), validate_output is captured with 'cat $validate_tmpfile' (no truncation limit) and injected raw into retry_extra_prompt. run_ac_verification() and the pre-flight check both apply OUTPUT_TRUNCATE_LINES truncation, but the VALIDATE_CMD retry path does not. For verbose test suites (cargo test, pytest, mocha), this produces thousands of lines in the retry prompt, degrading AI quality. The validator-agent retry path at line ~1769 has the same issue.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find _run_retry_loop() and locate the validate_output capture: validate_output=$(cat "$validate_tmpfile" 2>/dev/null || true)

Replace the cat with the same truncation pattern used in run_ac_verification(): capture wc -l, use tail -n $OUTPUT_TRUNCATE_LINES, prepend '[... output truncated, showing last N of M lines]' if truncated
Verify the validator-agent retry path also uses the truncated validate_output (line ~1769)
Confirm pre-flight check already has truncation (it does, around line 1650); no change needed there
acceptanceCriteria: VALIDATE_CMD output injected into retry prompts is truncated to OUTPUT_TRUNCATE_LINES lines
When output is truncated, the retry prompt includes a '[... output truncated, showing last N of M lines]' indicator
Truncation matches the pattern used in run_ac_verification() for consistency
items.groomingNotes: Same pattern as BUG-004 (AC verify truncation) but missed for the VALIDATE_CMD retry path. Fix is additive — apply OUTPUT_TRUNCATE_LINES truncation at one spot in _run_retry_loop(). Low risk, clear scope.

items.title: backlog edit has no --clear-ac flag to reset acceptance criteria
items.intent: Users must be able to reset AC via CLI — without --clear-ac, wrong AC entries require hand-editing JSON, violating the CLI-first design principle.
items.description: backlog edit --step has a companion --clear-steps flag; backlog edit --scope has a companion --clear-scope flag. But --ac has no --clear-ac. Once AC entries are added, the only way to remove them is to hand-edit the backlog JSON. This is inconsistent with the established pattern and violates CLI-first design. Fix: add --clear-ac to cmd_backlog_edit() that resets acceptanceCriteria to [], following the same pattern as --clear-steps.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_edit() and locate the --clear-steps case as reference (around line 3843-3846)
Add a --clear-ac case: set jq_updates with .acceptanceCriteria = [] and update update_desc
Add --clear-ac documentation to the help text in cmd_backlog_edit error message and cmd_help
Test: backlog edit BUG-003 --clear-ac (verify AC resets), then backlog edit BUG-003 --ac 'New' (verify fresh entry appears)
acceptanceCriteria: backlog edit <ID> --clear-ac resets acceptanceCriteria to []
backlog edit <ID> --clear-ac --ac 'New AC' results in only the new entry present
--clear-ac appears in the backlog edit help output
items.groomingNotes: Clear pattern mismatch — --clear-steps and --clear-scope both exist, --clear-ac does not. Fix is additive, one case block in cmd_backlog_edit plus help text update. Low risk.

items.title: remove_items_by_status takes full path while other CRUD helpers take relative filename
items.intent: Backlog CRUD helpers must use a consistent path convention — the current asymmetry is a latent footgun that will cause silent wrong-file operations when new callers are added.
items.description: update_item() and remove_item() accept a relative filename (e.g. 'backlog.json') and prepend BACKLOG_DIR internally. remove_items_by_status() takes a full path directly. Callers today are consistent, but the asymmetry is not documented and will cause bugs when someone adds a new call site with the wrong convention. Fix: change remove_items_by_status to accept a relative filename and prepend BACKLOG_DIR internally, matching the established pattern.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find remove_items_by_status() around line 703 and update it to accept a relative filename like update_item() and remove_item()
Change the jq command argument from '$file' to '$BACKLOG_DIR/$file'
Update cmd_clean() callers (line ~4025-4026) to pass just the filename ('backlog.json', 'bugs.json') instead of the full path variables
Run .aishore/aishore clean --dry-run and then clean on a test setup to confirm no regression
acceptanceCriteria: remove_items_by_status accepts a relative filename consistent with update_item and remove_item
cmd_clean still correctly removes done items after the refactor
grep confirms no other callers of remove_items_by_status exist with full paths
items.groomingNotes: Tech debt: path convention inconsistency between remove_items_by_status and the other CRUD helpers. Small refactor with one call site to update. Low risk — only one caller exists today (cmd_clean).

items.title: cmd_review silently ignores unknown options
items.intent: cmd_review must reject unknown flags immediately — silently discarding options lets typos pass undetected and erodes user trust in the CLI
items.description: cmd_review() parses --update-docs and --since but its catch-all branch is '* ) shift ;;' at line 2414, silently discarding any unrecognized flag. Most other commands (cmd_clean, cmd_backlog_add, etc.) reject unknown options with log_error and return 1. A user who mistypes '--update_docs' or '--since-commit' will get no error — the command runs silently in the wrong mode. Fix: replace the silent '* ) shift' with '* ) log_error "Unknown option: $1"; return 1' to match the established pattern.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_review() and locate the option parsing loop (around line 2410-2416)
Replace the catch-all '* ) shift ;;' with '* ) log_error "Unknown option: $1"; return 1 ;;'
Verify that valid options --update-docs and --since still work normally
Verify that 'aishore review --typo' now prints an error and exits non-zero
acceptanceCriteria: aishore review --unknown-flag exits with a non-zero status and prints an error message
aishore review --update-docs and aishore review --since <commit> still work correctly
Error message matches the format used by other commands: 'Unknown option: <flag>'
items.groomingNotes: Confirmed at line 2414: catch-all '* ) shift' drops unknown flags silently. Pattern inconsistency vs cmd_clean, cmd_backlog_add and others which all use log_error + return 1. One-line fix. Low risk — additive error handling only.

items.title: cmd_update silently ignores unknown options
items.intent: cmd_update must reject unknown flags — silently discarding options can make users believe a flag like --force or --dry-run took effect when it did not, potentially causing unintended updates
items.description: cmd_update() parses --dry-run, --check, --force, and --no-verify but its catch-all is '* ) shift ;;' at line 3174, silently discarding unknown flags. This is especially dangerous here: a user who mistypes '--dry-run' (e.g., '--dryrun' or '--dry_run') will get no error and the update will proceed as a live update — exactly the behavior they were trying to avoid. Fix: replace '* ) shift' with '* ) log_error "Unknown option: $1"; return 1' to match the established pattern in cmd_clean, cmd_backlog_add, etc.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_update() and locate the option parsing loop (around line 3169-3176)
Replace the catch-all '* ) shift ;;' with '* ) log_error "Unknown option: $1"; return 1 ;;'
Verify that valid options --dry-run, --force, --no-verify still work normally
Verify that 'aishore update --typo' now prints an error and exits non-zero
acceptanceCriteria: aishore update --unknown-flag exits with a non-zero status and prints an error message
aishore update --dry-run, --force, and --no-verify --force all still work correctly
Error message matches the format used by other commands: 'Unknown option: <flag>'
items.groomingNotes: Bumped to must: a mistyped --dry-run (e.g. --dryrun or --dry_run) silently executes a live update — the user explicitly tried to preview and got destructive action with no warning. This is the same class of failure as BUG-005 (silent config ignore). update is a state-changing, potentially irreversible operation; the --dry-run flag is a safety net that must be reliable. One-line fix, but high consequence if missed.

items.title: cmd_groom and cmd_metrics silently accept unknown options
items.intent: All CLI commands must reject unknown flags — silent acceptance of garbage input makes the CLI unpredictable and violates the established error-handling pattern
items.description: cmd_groom() at line 2262 checks only '${1:-}' == '--backlog' without a proper arg-parsing loop, so any extra argument after --backlog is silently ignored. cmd_metrics() at line 2470 does the same: checks only '${1:-}' == '--json'. Neither reports an error for unknown flags like '--bogus'. Both should use a proper while/case loop with a catch-all that calls log_error and returns 1, consistent with cmd_clean, cmd_backlog_add, and other commands in the same script.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_groom() (line 2258) and replace the single-line check '[\[ "${1:-}" == "--backlog" ]]' with a proper while/case loop that handles --backlog and rejects unknown options
In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_metrics() (line 2466) and replace the single-line check '[\[ "${1:-}" == "--json" ]]' with a proper while/case loop that handles --json and rejects unknown options
Verify that 'aishore groom --backlog' and 'aishore groom' still work correctly
Verify that 'aishore metrics --json' and 'aishore metrics' still work correctly
Verify that 'aishore groom --typo' and 'aishore metrics --typo' now print an error and exit non-zero
acceptanceCriteria: aishore groom --unknown-flag exits with a non-zero status and prints 'Unknown option: --unknown-flag'
aishore metrics --unknown-flag exits with a non-zero status and prints 'Unknown option: --unknown-flag'
aishore groom, aishore groom --backlog, aishore metrics, and aishore metrics --json all continue to work correctly
items.groomingNotes: Both cmd_groom (line 2262) and cmd_metrics (line 2470) use single-check pattern instead of proper while/case loop. Lower severity than BUG-011/012 since these commands have fewer dangerous flags, but still violates CLI consistency. Two functions to fix, same pattern each. Low risk.

items.title: cmd_backlog_populate silently ignores unknown options
items.intent: cmd_backlog_populate must reject unknown flags — silently discarding options is inconsistent with the established error-handling pattern and hides user mistakes
items.description: cmd_backlog_populate() (around line 3457) performs no option parsing at all. Any flag passed to 'backlog populate' is silently ignored. Most other commands use a while/case loop with a catch-all that calls log_error and returns 1. Fix: add a proper option parsing loop. populate currently takes no flags, so any argument is an error — the loop body should be just the catch-all that rejects everything.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_populate() and identify the current argument handling (none)
Add a while/case loop before the function body that rejects unknown options with log_error 'Unknown option: $1' and returns 1
Verify that 'backlog populate' with no arguments still works correctly
Verify that 'backlog populate --typo' now prints an error and exits non-zero
acceptanceCriteria: backlog populate --unknown-flag exits with a non-zero status and prints 'Unknown option: --unknown-flag'
backlog populate with no arguments still works correctly
Error message matches the format used by other commands
items.groomingNotes: No option parsing at all in cmd_backlog_populate. Fix is additive — add a while/case loop with a catch-all that rejects all arguments since populate takes no flags. Low risk, same pattern as BUG-011/012/013.

items.title: cmd_run treats unknown flags as item IDs instead of erroring
items.intent: cmd_run must reject unrecognized flags — silently accepting unknown options as item IDs causes confusing behavior where typos like --dry_run are interpreted as sprint IDs and silently do nothing
items.description: cmd_run() has a catch-all in its option parsing loop (around line 1905) that accepts any non-flag positional argument as either a count or a specific item ID. This means unknown flags like '--dry_run' (intended as --dry-run) or '--retries' (missing value) are silently interpreted as item IDs, producing a 'no item found' error rather than 'unknown option'. The fix is to detect that an argument starts with '--' in the catch-all and reject it as an unknown option before treating it as a positional.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_run() and locate the catch-all in the option parsing loop (around line 1905-1912)
In the catch-all branch, before the existing logic, add: if [[ "$1" == --* ]]; then log_error "Unknown option: $1"; return 1; fi
Verify that 'run 3', 'run FEAT-001', and 'run' without arguments still work correctly
Verify that 'run --typo' now prints an error and exits non-zero
acceptanceCriteria: aishore run --unknown-flag exits with a non-zero status and prints an error message
aishore run N (numeric count), aishore run <ID>, and aishore run still work correctly
Error message matches the format used by other commands: 'Unknown option: <flag>'
items.groomingNotes: Catch-all in cmd_run interprets --flag-typos as item IDs. Fix is a single guard in the catch-all: check if arg starts with '--' and reject it. Low risk — only changes error behavior, not normal paths.

items.title: cmd_backlog_edit usage message omits most supported flags
items.intent: The backlog edit help text must list all supported flags — a usage message that omits 9 of 18 flags is misleading and makes the CLI appear less capable than it is
items.description: cmd_backlog_edit() has two help text locations that list supported flags: the early usage error message (around line 3767) and the catch-all error at the end (around line 3941). The early message lists only: --title, --intent, --priority, --status, --desc, --category, --depends-on, --ready, --no-ready. It omits: --step, --clear-steps, --scope, --clear-scope, --ac, --ac-verify, --clear-ac, --groomed-at, --groomed-notes. The catch-all message at line 3941 is complete. Fix: update the early usage message to match the complete list in the catch-all.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_edit() and locate the early usage error message (around line 3767) that is shown when no ID is provided
Compare it to the complete flag list in the catch-all error message (around line 3941)
Update the early usage message to include all supported flags: --step, --clear-steps, --scope, --clear-scope, --ac, --ac-verify, --clear-ac, --groomed-at, --groomed-notes
Verify that 'backlog edit' with no arguments shows the updated usage message
acceptanceCriteria: Running 'backlog edit' with no arguments shows a usage message that includes --step, --clear-steps, --ac, --ac-verify, --clear-ac, --scope, --clear-scope, --groomed-at, --groomed-notes
The complete flag list in the catch-all error message is unchanged
No functional behavior changes — this is display only
items.groomingNotes: PO bump to should: flags like --step, --clear-steps, --ac, --ac-verify, --clear-ac were added specifically to enable CLI-first grooming without JSON editing. Hiding 9 of 18 flags in the early usage message undermines the discoverability of that entire investment. Users who type 'backlog edit' to learn the options will think those features don't exist. This is a documentation debt that defeats the purpose of BUG-006, BUG-007, and BUG-009.

items.title: cmd_backlog_add missing validate_arg calls for most flags
items.intent: backlog add must validate that flag arguments are non-empty — silently accepting empty values creates malformed backlog items that fail sprint readiness checks without a clear error message
items.description: cmd_backlog_add() calls validate_arg for --step (line 3617) but not for --type, --title, --intent, --desc, --priority, --category, or --depends-on (lines 3592-3600). This means 'backlog add --title' (with no value) silently sets title to an empty string, producing a malformed item. By contrast, cmd_backlog_edit() consistently calls validate_arg for all value-bearing flags. Fix: add validate_arg calls in cmd_backlog_add() to match the pattern already established in cmd_backlog_edit().
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_add() and locate the flag handling block (around lines 3592-3617)

For each flag that takes a value (--type, --title, --intent, --desc, --priority, --category, --depends-on), add validate_arg "$1" "${2:-}" || return 1 before the assignment, matching the pattern in cmd_backlog_edit()
Verify that 'backlog add --title' (no value) now prints an error and exits non-zero
Verify that 'backlog add --title "My Item" --priority must' still creates the item correctly
acceptanceCriteria: backlog add --title (with no value) exits with a non-zero status and prints an error
backlog add --intent (with no value) exits with a non-zero status and prints an error
backlog add with all required values still creates items correctly
validate_arg is called for --type, --title, --intent, --desc, --priority, --category, and --depends-on
items.groomingNotes: cmd_backlog_edit already validates all flags with validate_arg; cmd_backlog_add inconsistently skips it for most flags. Additive fix — add validate_arg calls. Low risk, small scope.

items.title: cmd_backlog_show and cmd_backlog_check silently accept extra positional arguments
items.intent: backlog show and backlog check must reject extra arguments — silently ignoring them makes 'backlog show FEAT-001 FEAT-002' appear to succeed while only processing the first ID, giving users false confidence
items.description: cmd_backlog_show() extracts $1 as the item ID and ignores anything after it. cmd_backlog_check() does the same. Both commands silently accept extra arguments: 'backlog show FEAT-001 FEAT-002' processes only FEAT-001 without any error, which is misleading. The fix for each is to check that exactly one positional argument was given and reject anything extra with log_error.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_show() and after extracting the ID from $1, check if $# -gt 1 and emit log_error 'Unexpected argument: ${2}' and return 1
In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_check() and apply the same fix — after extracting the ID, check for extra arguments and error
Verify that 'backlog show FEAT-001' and 'backlog check FEAT-001' still work correctly
Verify that 'backlog show FEAT-001 FEAT-002' and 'backlog check FEAT-001 --typo' now print an error and exit non-zero
acceptanceCriteria: backlog show <ID> <extra> exits non-zero and prints an error about the unexpected argument
backlog check <ID> <extra> exits non-zero and prints an error about the unexpected argument
backlog show <ID> and backlog check <ID> with exactly one argument still work correctly
items.groomingNotes: Both cmd_backlog_show and cmd_backlog_check silently drop extra positional args. Additive check: if $# > 1 after extracting ID, error out. Low risk, two functions to fix.

items.title: cmd_backlog_check returns 0 when readiness gates fail
items.intent: backlog check must return non-zero when gates fail — callers and scripts must be able to rely on exit status to detect unready items.
items.description: cmd_backlog_check() calls check_readiness_gates() and logs warnings on failure, but does not return 1. The function exits 0 regardless of gate outcome. Any script using 'backlog check $ID && do_something' will proceed even when gates fail, undermining the purpose of the check command. Fix: add 'return 1' in the else branch after printing gate warnings.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_check() (around line 3456)
After the 'echo -e "$CHECK_GATES_WARNINGS"' line in the else branch, add 'return 1'
Verify that 'backlog check <ready-ID>' exits 0 and 'backlog check <unready-ID>' exits non-zero
acceptanceCriteria: backlog check <ID> exits non-zero when any readiness gate fails
backlog check <ID> exits 0 when all readiness gates pass
Exit status matches the log output — if warnings are printed, exit is non-zero
items.groomingNotes: Clear behavioral bug: gates failing but exit 0 is wrong. One-line fix — add return 1 in else branch. Low risk, high value for automation users.

items.title: cmd_backlog_edit flushes pending AC entry without JSON encoding
items.intent: AC entries appended via --ac must be correctly JSON-encoded — unencoded special characters silently produce invalid JSON and corrupt the backlog.
items.description: In cmd_backlog_edit(), when a second --ac flag is encountered, the pending AC entry from the prior --ac is flushed to ac_entries with 'ac_entries+=("$pending_ac")' (line ~3898) — no JSON encoding. cmd_backlog_add() correctly uses 'ac_entries+=(""\"$pending_ac\""")' in the same flush path. If the AC text contains '"', backslash, or other JSON metacharacters, the constructed JSON will be invalid, silently corrupting the item's acceptanceCriteria. The final-flush path at line ~3942 IS correctly encoded — only the mid-loop flush is broken.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_edit() and locate the --ac case (around line 3894)
Find the flush block: 'if [[ -n "$pending_ac" ]]; then ac_entries+=("$pending_ac"); fi'
Replace the flush with the encoded form matching cmd_backlog_add: 'ac_entries+=("\"\"$pending_ac\""")'
Verify: 'backlog edit <ID> --ac 'has "quotes"' --ac 'second entry'' produces valid JSON in the backlog file
acceptanceCriteria: backlog edit <ID> --ac 'text with "quotes"' --ac 'second' stores both entries with proper JSON encoding
backlog edit with plain AC entries (no special chars) still works correctly after the fix
items.groomingNotes: PO bump to must: silent JSON corruption of backlog data when AC text contains quotes/backslash is in the same class as BUG-005 — user does the right thing, gets wrong behavior, no signal. A corrupted item fails at sprint time with a cryptic error, not at edit time where the fix is obvious. The mid-loop flush is the single broken path; final-flush is correct. One-line fix with high consequence if left unresolved.

items.title: backlog list --status and --type flags missing validate_arg for empty values
items.intent: backlog list must reject flag calls with missing values — silently accepting an empty filter silently changes which items are shown, leading to misleading output.
items.description: In cmd_backlog_list(), the --status and --type flag cases do 'filter_status="$2"; shift 2' without calling validate_arg first. If a user runs 'backlog list --status' without a value, $2 is empty, filter_status gets set to empty string, and the subsequent jq filter produces unexpected results. cmd_backlog_add and cmd_backlog_edit consistently call validate_arg for all value-bearing flags. The fix is to add validate_arg calls in the --status and --type cases, consistent with the established pattern.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_list() and locate the --status and --type flag cases (around line 3546-3552)
Add 'validate_arg "$1" "${2:-}" || return 1' before each assignment, matching the pattern in cmd_backlog_add/edit
Verify that 'backlog list --status' (no value) prints an error and exits non-zero
Verify that 'backlog list --type' (no value) prints an error and exits non-zero
Verify that 'backlog list --status todo' and 'backlog list --type bug' still work correctly
acceptanceCriteria: backlog list --status (no value) exits non-zero with an error message
backlog list --type (no value) exits non-zero with an error message
backlog list --status todo and backlog list --type bug still work correctly
items.groomingNotes: Same pattern as BUG-017 (cmd_backlog_add missing validate_arg) but for cmd_backlog_list. Two flag cases need validate_arg added. Low risk, additive only.

items.title: cmd_clean prints success even when remove_items_by_status fails
items.intent: cmd_clean must not report successful cleanup if the backlog file was not actually updated — silent data loss on I/O error is worse than a visible failure.
items.description: In cmd_clean(), remove_items_by_status() is called but its return code is not checked. If jq fails (malformed JSON, disk full) or the mv that replaces the file fails, the command continues and prints 'Cleaned N features/bugs' as though it succeeded. The user believes the backlog was cleaned when it was not. Fix: check return code of both remove_items_by_status calls and exit non-zero with a clear error if either fails.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_clean() and locate the two remove_items_by_status calls (around line 4067-4068)
Capture the return code of each call: 'remove_items_by_status ... || { log_error "Failed to clean backlog.json"; return 1; }'
Ensure the success message is only printed after both calls succeed
Verify: run clean on a valid setup (success printed), then test with a read-only backlog file (error printed, no success message)
acceptanceCriteria: cmd_clean exits non-zero and prints an error when remove_items_by_status fails
cmd_clean success message is only printed when cleanup actually succeeds
cmd_clean --dry-run is not affected (no file writes in dry-run path)
items.groomingNotes: Silent success on write failure is the same class as BUG-005 (user does right thing, gets wrong behavior, no signal). The return code is not checked after remove_items_by_status — add || return 1 guard on both calls. Low risk, additive error handling only.

items.title: mark_item_failed silently swallows update errors with || true
items.intent: Sprint failure context must be recorded reliably — if mark_item_failed silently does nothing, subsequent retries and auto mode have no failure history to work from.
items.description: In mark_item_failed(), the call to update_item() is followed by '2>/dev/null || true', which suppresses all errors and always succeeds. If the backlog file write fails (bad JSON, permissions, missing file), the item is never marked failed and no error is surfaced. Auto mode and retry logic read failCount and lastFailReason from the item — if mark_item_failed silently no-ops, those fields are never set, degrading sprint recovery. Fix: remove || true, keep stderr suppression only when source_file is empty (item lookup already failed), and log a warning if update_item returns non-zero.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find mark_item_failed() (around line 1355)
Locate the update_item call with '2>/dev/null || true'
Split the logic: if source_file is empty (item not found), it is expected to fail — log_warn and return 0

Otherwise, call update_item without || true; on failure, emit log_warn 'Failed to record sprint failure for $item_id' but still return 0 (best-effort — do not abort the sprint just because the metadata write failed)
Verify: mark_item_failed on a known item updates failCount; mark_item_failed on an unknown ID logs a warning
acceptanceCriteria: mark_item_failed logs a warning when update_item fails instead of silently succeeding
mark_item_failed on a known item correctly sets failCount and lastFailReason
Sprint execution continues even if mark_item_failed cannot write (best-effort semantics preserved)
items.groomingNotes: || true suppresses all errors including legitimate write failures. Best-effort semantics should still apply (don't abort sprint on metadata write failure), but we should warn. Split: empty source_file case (item not found = expected, keep silent) vs write failure case (unexpected = log_warn). Low risk, additive warning only.

items.title: backlog add --ac-verify missing validate_arg allows empty verify command
items.intent: backlog add must reject --ac-verify without a value — an empty verify command silently creates a broken AC entry that fails at sprint verification time with no clear error.
items.description: In cmd_backlog_add(), all value-bearing flags call validate_arg except --ac-verify (lines 3652-3659). The companion cmd_backlog_edit() correctly calls validate_arg for --ac-verify at line 3915. Without the guard, 'backlog add --ac "check it" --ac-verify' (no value) either causes an unbound variable crash under set -u or silently creates {"text":"check it","verify":""} with an empty verify command. At sprint time, the empty verify command fails with a confusing error rather than at edit time where the fix is obvious.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_add() and locate the --ac-verify case (around line 3652)
Add validate_arg "$1" "${2:-}" || return 1 before the if [[ -z "$pending_ac" ]] check, matching line 3915 in cmd_backlog_edit()
Verify: 'backlog add --ac "check" --ac-verify' (no value) prints error and exits non-zero
Verify: 'backlog add --ac "check" --ac-verify "make test"' still creates the correct AC entry
acceptanceCriteria: backlog add --ac 'text' --ac-verify (no value) exits non-zero and prints an error
backlog add --ac 'text' --ac-verify 'cmd' still works correctly
validate_arg is called for --ac-verify consistent with cmd_backlog_edit
items.groomingNotes: Missing validate_arg in --ac-verify in cmd_backlog_add is the last validate_arg gap after BUG-017 fixed the others. cmd_backlog_edit already has validate_arg for --ac-verify at line 3915 — add the same call one line before the pending_ac check in cmd_backlog_add. One-line fix, low risk. Steps and AC are concrete.

items.title: cmd_review --since flag missing validate_arg for empty value
items.intent: cmd_review must reject --since without a value — silently consuming the next flag as a commit hash causes the review to run with wrong scope, giving users false confidence the review covered the intended range.
items.description: In cmd_review(), the --since flag case at line 2428 does 'since="$2"; shift 2' with no validate_arg call. If the user runs 'aishore review --since' without a commit hash, $2 is either empty or the next flag (e.g. --update-docs). Under set -u, an empty $2 accessed as a positional parameter would cause an unbound variable error; but since $2 is accessed inside a case branch where shift already happened, it just silently sets since to "" or the wrong value. All other value-bearing flags across the codebase call validate_arg first. Compare with cmd_backlog_history --since at lines 4019-4025, which correctly validates the value before using it.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_review() and locate the --since case at line 2428
Add validate_arg "$1" "${2:-}" || return 1 before the assignment, matching the pattern used in cmd_backlog_edit()
Verify that 'aishore review --since' (no value) now exits non-zero with an error
Verify that 'aishore review --since abc123' and 'aishore review --since abc --update-docs' still work
acceptanceCriteria: aishore review --since (no value) exits non-zero with a clear error message
aishore review --since <commit> and aishore review --since --update-docs still work correctly
Error message matches the validate_arg pattern used by other flags
items.groomingNotes: Same class as BUG-021 (missing validate_arg for value-bearing flag). The cmd_review --since case is the only remaining value-bearing flag without validate_arg in cmd_review. One-line fix. Compare with cmd_backlog_history --since which correctly validates at lines 4019-4025. Low risk, additive only.

items.title: backlog edit --groomed-at accepts invalid date values without validation
items.intent: backlog edit --groomed-at must reject non-date values — silently storing garbage in groomedAt corrupts item metadata and is inconsistent with backlog history --since which validates the same YYYY-MM-DD format.
items.description: In cmd_backlog_edit(), the --groomed-at case at lines 3934-3945 accepts any non-empty value that doesn't start with '--' as a valid date. There is no format check. Running 'backlog edit FEAT-001 --groomed-at notadate' silently stores 'notadate' in groomedAt. In contrast, cmd_backlog_history() validates its --since date argument against ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}$ before using it (lines 4022-4024). The groomedAt field is displayed in backlog show and used for tracking grooming activity, so invalid values undermine tooling built on this field. Fix: add the same regex validation used in cmd_backlog_history() --since.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_edit() and locate the --groomed-at case (around line 3934)
After reading gdate from $2, add a format check: if \! [[ "$gdate" =~ ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}$ ]]; then log_error "Invalid date format: $gdate (expected YYYY-MM-DD)"; return 1; fi
This check should only apply when the user provided a value (shift 2 path), not the default-to-today path (shift 1 path)
Verify: 'backlog edit <ID> --groomed-at baddate' errors; 'backlog edit <ID> --groomed-at 2026-01-01' works; 'backlog edit <ID> --groomed-at' sets today's date
acceptanceCriteria: backlog edit <ID> --groomed-at notadate exits non-zero and prints an error about invalid date format
backlog edit <ID> --groomed-at 2026-03-20 still sets groomedAt correctly
backlog edit <ID> --groomed-at (no value) still uses today's date as the default
Error message includes expected format: YYYY-MM-DD
items.groomingNotes: Pattern inconsistency: cmd_backlog_history validates --since date format with ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}$ at lines 4022-4024; cmd_backlog_edit --groomed-at does no format check. Add the same regex validation in the shift-2 (user-provided value) path only — the default-to-today path is already correct. Low risk, additive. Note: --groomed-at accepts optional value so the validate_arg pattern doesn't apply — must validate after reading.

items.title: Stash pop fails silently on conflicts after sprint failure
items.intent: Users must always know the state of their uncommitted changes. Silent data loss is unacceptable.
steps: Stop suppressing stderr from git stash pop
On pop failure, run git stash show and display output
Use log_warning with actionable guidance
In EXIT trap, apply same visible-failure treatment
acceptanceCriteria: When stash pop fails, stderr is visible
log_warning shows stashed files
Warning includes actionable guidance
Normal stash pop still works silently

items.title: Commit messages are generic — agent summaries discarded
items.intent: Every commit message must describe what changed and why. Generic messages make git log useless.
steps: Capture developer agent summary from result.json
Use agent summary as safety-net commit message body
Use agent summary in merge commit message
Fall back to generic message if no summary
Truncate long summaries to 500 chars
acceptanceCriteria: Safety-net commit includes developer summary
Merge commit includes agent summary
Falls back to template if no summary
Follows conventional commit format

items.title: Grooming agents generate unbounded busywork with no stopping condition
items.intent: Grooming must converge. Agents must stop when returns diminish.
steps: Add groom_max_items config default 10
Add groom_min_priority config threshold default should
Instruct grooming agent to skip low-value items
Log summary of items added vs skipped
Enforce limits in auto-groom trigger
acceptanceCriteria: Grooming stops after groom_max_items per session
Items below groom_min_priority logged but not added
Default cap of 10 items
